text: Welcome to The Long View. Today we're exploring continuous glucose monitors and what they tell you in depth with Dr. Elena Fischer, researcher in metabolic and longevity science.
Thanks, Alex Morgan. This is a topic I've spent decades on.

This episode is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Get started at linear.com/podcast.
Why is systems thinking the part that most analyses get wrong?

The short answer is CGMs are diagnostic tools more than they are training tools. Teams working with WHOOP run into this constantly. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Is there a counterexample that would change your view?

That ties to another point — I changed my mind on CGM use for general population — turns out 80% gain at 5% of intervention cost is wellness, not biohacking. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Let's talk about risk management. What's your read?

Honestly, Most postprandial glucose 'spikes' are noise to anyone without metabolic dysfunction. Teams working with Levels run into this constantly. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
That ties to another point — CGMs are diagnostic tools more than they are training tools. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
What's the deepest piece of context on reliability that listeners are missing?

I'd put it this way: The systems-thinking version of CGM use is feedback loops, not gamification. Teams working with InsideTracker run into this constantly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
